straight_beat.name: Mickey Dougherty
straight_beat.column:
The Phillies lost yesterday, but not in a way that feels like a dead-end street. They lost to the Cubs, a team that’s been struggling to stay afloat in the NL Central, and yet the way they did it—crushing the Cubs 13-7—felt like a reminder of what this team is capable of. It’s the kind of game that makes you wonder: What if they had a little more consistency? What if they didn’t let a few bad outings derail their momentum?  

The pitching staff, for all its flaws, showed flashes of the potential that got them here. Jake Arrieta, for instance, was dominant in the first inning, striking out five and keeping the Cubs

optimist.name: Terri 'Terry' Pascucci
optimist.column:
The Cubs handed us a 13-7 beatdown yesterday, but let’s not get too down. You know what I say when the sun goes down? “Ring the bell, babe.” Not the one in the subway, the one in my head. The one that goes off every time the Phillies take the field, reminding me that every loss is just a setup for a bigger win. That’s why I’ve got that mural on the side of my cheesesteak stand—graffiti, actually, done by a kid who’s got a thing for Phillies jerseys. It’s got a cannon pointing at the sky, and the words “Next Week” in bold. You ever seen a mural like that? It’s a promise.  

Now, I know the numbers don’t look great—8-8, 1.5 games back. But let’s talk about the cannon. Schwarber went 0-for-5, yeah, but that’s four home runs saved up in the cannon. By Sunday this place is gonna sound like October. You think the Cubs are the only ones with a plan? We’ve got a front office that’s been moving pieces like it’s a chess game, and I’m telling you, they’re setting up for a rally.  

Look

pessimist.name: Vinnie DePasquale
pessimist.column:
The Cubs came to town yesterday, and the Phillies didn’t even try to act like they were in a game. They were just going through the motions, handing the ball to a bullpen that’s been a joke since the day they drafted the last guy with a name that rhymes with “drought.” Seven runs in the first inning? That’s not a lead, that’s a wake-up call. And yet, here we are, still staring at the same scoreboard, still wondering when this season is going to finally decide to start.  

You think the bullpen’s the only problem? The offense had a moment, sure—eight runs in the sixth, like they remembered how to hit a fastball. But then they went and gave it all back in the seventh, because that’s what they do. The bats have the power, but they’ve got zero sense of urgency. You can’t hit a home run and then walk four batters in the next inning. That’s not baseball; that’s a bad dream. And if you’re dreaming, you’re not winning.  

And let’s talk about the manager. You know what he’s doing? He’s making decisions that make you want to throw your coffee across the room. Pinch-hitting a guy who’s hit .180 since the start of last season? That’s not strategy, that’s a dare. And the lineup? It’s like someone took a deck of cards, shuffled them, and decided to play 52 different games in one afternoon. You can’t expect a .230 average to carry you when you’re trailing by five in the
